votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the length check and the element-wise increment check specified in the Coq Prop.
The Python code correctly checks that the lengths are equal and that each element in 'res' is the increment of the corresponding element in 'l', matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the length check and the element-wise increment condition specified in the Coq Prop.

The Python code correctly checks that res has the same length as l and that each element res[i] equals l[i] + 1, which matches the Coq specification that requires equal lengths and nth_error res i = Some (x + 1) when nth_error l i = Some x. For indices beyond the list length, both nth_error calls return None in Coq, satisfying None = None, which Python handles implicitly by only iterating over valid indices.

The Python code correctly checks that lengths are equal and that each element in res equals the corresponding element in l plus 1. For indices within bounds, it verifies res[i] == l[i] + 1, matching the Coq spec's Some(x+1) case. For out-of-bounds indices, both nth_error calls return None in Coq, which is trivially satisfied and doesn't need explicit checking in Python since the loop only covers valid indices.

For indices beyond the list length, both nth_error calls return None in Coq, and the Python code doesn't need to check those explicitly since it only iterates over valid indices.
It enforces equal lengths and element-wise res[i] = l[i] + 1, which matches the Coq nth_error condition for all indices (out-of-range indices yield None on both).
It checks equal lengths and that each res[i] equals l[i]+1 for all in-bounds indices; with equal lengths, out-of-bounds nth_error values are None on both sides, matching the Coq spec.
Checks equal lengths and per-index increment; with equal lengths, out-of-bounds indices map to None on both sides, matching the Coq nth_error-based spec for all concrete lists of naturals.
